content:
4 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

It feels incredibly good to press Tab and watch GitHub Copilot spit out a perfectly formatted 50-line React component in a fraction of a second. It feels like magic. It feels like the future.
But if we aren’t careful, it is also a trap.
In my previous article on how AI is breaking the developer pipeline, I talked about the macro-level industry problem: companies aren’t hiring juniors, which cuts off the lifecycle of future senior engineers. Today, I want to talk about the micro-level problem — the one happening right inside your IDE.
We are facing an epidemic of “cognitive atrophy” in software engineering. When you outsource the struggle of debugging, state management, and algorithmic thinking to an LLM, you are outsourcing your own growth.
If you want to survive and thrive in this new era, you need to establish strict boundaries with your AI tools. Here are my four rules of engagement for using AI without losing your software engineering soul.
The Cognitive Atrophy Trap
Building software is hard. It requires a specific type of mental elasticity that is only forged through frustration. The hours spent wrestling with a stubborn API response or a weird React re-render cycle are exactly how developers build “muscle memory.”
When you bypass that struggle entirely, your brain gets lazy. You stop thinking about how systems connect and start acting as a copy-paste middleman between ChatGPT and VS Code. Eventually, when a complex, production-breaking bug occurs that AI cannot solve, you will find yourself completely paralyzed.
To prevent this, you have to treat AI as what it is: a co-pilot, not an autopilot.
Rule 1: Never merge code you cannot explain line-by-line.
It’s tempting to let AI write a complex Regex pattern or an intricate SQL query, shrug, and say, “Well, it works.”
Never do this. If an AI writes a block of code, you must be able to stand in front of a whiteboard and explain its logic to your team. If it introduces a subtle security vulnerability or a massive memory leak in production, “the AI wrote it” will not save your job. You are the engineer; you hold the liability.
Rule 2: Outsource the typing, not the architecture.
AI is phenomenal at tedious tasks: mapping over arrays, generating boilerplate layouts, or writing repetitive CSS/Tailwind classes. Let it handle the grunt work.
But reserve your human brainpower for the high-level architecture: system design, component hierarchy, security, and data flow.
For example, when I was building a complex Electronic Medical Record (EMR) system for a public hospital, I used AI to speed up the mundane UI boilerplate. But I relied entirely on my own engineering fundamentals to structure the React state management and handle the critical patient-data architecture. You cannot afford to let an LLM hallucinate logic when real-world stakes — like healthcare data — are involved.
Rule 3: Use AI as a tutor, not just a generator.
Change the way you prompt. Instead of asking, “Write a function to sort this data by date,” try asking, “Explain the most efficient algorithm to sort this specific data structure in JavaScript, and give me a small hint so I can write it myself.”
This simple shift changes AI from a crutch into a personalized senior mentor. It forces you to engage with the logic, ensuring that your core coding skills stay sharp while still getting the answers you need quickly.
Rule 4: Break the testing echo chamber.
If AI writes your component and you ask it to write the unit tests for that same component, you are creating a dangerous feedback loop. The AI will often simply validate its own flawed logic.
If the AI misunderstood the edge cases during implementation, it will ignore those same edge cases in the tests. Always write your own test cases, or at the very least, heavily audit and expand upon the ones the AI generates to ensure they test actual business requirements, not just the code’s syntax.
The Bottom Line
AI is not going to replace software engineers, but software engineers who use AI poorly will be replaced by those who use it with discipline.
Protect your struggle. Protect your foundational knowledge. Let the AI type, but make sure you are always the one driving.
